ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for cha chaan teng luncheon meat and egg toast set. FOODON_00002473 (breakfast food) used as closest parent category.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (composite dish) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for this Hong Kong-style breakfast set.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific cha chaan teng breakfast combination.

serving.description: 1 set (luncheon meat slices + 2 fried eggs + 2 slices toast)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising two slices of pan-fried luncheon meat, two fried eggs, and two slices of buttered white toast, as typically served in a Singapore cha chaan teng or Hong Kong-style café.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo component data: luncheon meat (pork), fried egg, and white bread with butter
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (supplementary)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for the complete cha chaan teng luncheon meat and double egg toast set. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for: (1) luncheon meat / pork sausage, (2) fried egg, and (3) white bread with butter, weighted by typical serving proportions (luncheon meat ~80 g, 2 eggs ~120 g, 2 slices toast with butter ~100 g, total ~300 g edible portion, scaled to per-100 g). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used to cross-check luncheon meat and egg values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white bread and mixed egg-meat composite meals — analogous estimate based on white bread GI (GI=70) and mixed protein-fat attenuation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this cha chaan teng set. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: white bread GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database), attenuated by the significant protein (luncheon meat, eggs) and fat content of the meal, which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 56.2) / 100 ≈ 36.6, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 14.8 × 3.8 = 56.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from luncheon meat and processed bread
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of this set provides approximately 2,356 mg sodium (620 mg per 100 g × 3.8), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish and request reduced-sodium alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined white bread, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (65) and high glycemic load per serving (~36.6) from refined white toast make this set a moderate concern for blood glucose management; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting wholegrain toast, reducing portion size, or pairing with a low-GI beverage to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, cholesterol, sodium, and trans fat from luncheon meat and frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in saturated fat (~4.6 g/100 g), cholesterol (~185 mg/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and sodium (~620 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption frequency and consider lower-fat protein substitutes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 肠仔煎双蛋（配多士）, romanized as Cheung Jai Jian Seung Dan Pei Do Si, is a classic Hong Kong-style cha chaan teng (茶餐廳) breakfast set that has become a staple in Singapore's Chinese café culture. The dish consists of two slices of pan-fried luncheon meat (canned pork sausage, locally known as 肠仔 or cheung jai), two pan-fried or sunny-side-up eggs, and two slices of thick-cut white bread toast spread with butter or margarine. The luncheon meat is typically sliced from a can of Spam or a similar brand, pan-fried in oil until golden and slightly crisp on the outside while remaining soft inside. The eggs are fried in the same pan, often with runny yolks. The toast is lightly buttered and served alongside or beneath the protein components. The overall flavour profile is savoury, rich, and slightly salty, with a contrast of textures between the crispy luncheon meat, soft egg, and crunchy toast. Nutritionally, this set is energy-dense (~218 kcal/100 g), high in protein (~10.8 g/100 g), moderate-to-high in fat (~13.2 g/100 g), and contains significant sodium (~620 mg/100 g) primarily from the processed luncheon meat. The dish carries a moderate glycemic index (~65) due to the refined white bread, though the protein and fat content attenuate the postprandial glucose response. Clinically, the high sodium and saturated fat content make this a high-concern dish for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or brunch item and is widely available at Singapore kopitiams, cha chaan tengs, and Hong Kong-style cafés. The dish is not halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this exact cha chaan teng set. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo component data for luncheon meat, fried egg, and buttered white toast, weighted by typical serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, attenuated for protein and fat. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Singapore Foodo component entries for luncheon meat (pork), fried egg, and buttered white bread, scaled to per-100 g of the combined dish. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. Sodium is high primarily due to the processed luncheon meat component; actual sodium may vary by brand (e.g., Spam vs. generic). 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for luncheon meat and eggs; these values are approximate. 5. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 6. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical cha chaan teng portion sizes observed in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary. 7. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA SR Legacy cross-reference; no -1 values remain in per_100g.
